description: Figure 26 displays engineering stress–strain curves for two ferritic steels, Fe-6.8Al and Fe-8.1Al (850°C/5 min [41], red curves), and two ferrite-based duplex steels, Fe-3.3Mn-6.6Al-0.2C [44] (blue curves) and Fe-8.5Mn-5.6Al-0.3C [51] (black curves), after different heat treatments. The ferritic steels show the lowest strength, with ultimate tensile strength (UTS) around 450–500 MPa and total elongation (TE) of roughly 15–30%. The Fe-3.3Mn-6.6Al-0.2C steel exhibits moderate strength and enhanced elongation when austempered at 400°C/10 min after 800°C/5 min, which the caption attributes to a microstructure mixture of δ-ferrite, bainite, martensite, and retained austenite. The Fe-8.5Mn-5.6Al-0.3C steel achieves the highest performance, reaching approximately 850 MPa UTS and up to about 70% strain under 800°C/1 min, 800°C/30 min, and 900°C/30 min annealing. The text reports that the TRIP mechanism is operative in the 800°C-annealed samples, whereas simultaneous TRIP and TWIP occur in the 900°C/30 min condition, enabling these duplex steels to deliver a superior strength–ductility balance of 600–950 MPa tensile strength and 30–70% total elongation.
